agent_output: {"domain_analysis":{"business_context":"Proposed change to auto-delete delivery records after 90 days, down from 7-year retention. Framed as infrastructure optimization with no business logic changes. Platform operates in EU and UK markets serving both B2C consumers and B2B logistics companies. B2B customers depend on delivery data for their own compliance reporting.","bounded_context":"Delivery Record Management / Data Lifecycle — intersects with Compliance, Billing, Customer Reporting, and Dispute Resolution bounded contexts.","ubiquitous_language":[{"term":"Delivery Record","definition":"The complete data artifact capturing a delivery lifecycle: creation, pickup, transit events, proof of delivery, exceptions, and final disposition. Serves as the legal record of service fulfillment."},{"term":"Proof of Delivery (POD)","definition":"Evidence that a shipment was delivered to the correct recipient — signatures, photos, GPS coordinates, timestamps. Legally required for dispute resolution and often contractually required for B2B customers."},{"term":"Data Retention Policy","definition":"The rules governing how long specific categories of data are stored, driven by legal obligations, contractual commitments, and business needs — not by storage cost preferences."},{"term":"Compliance Reporting","definition":"The process by which B2B customers use delivery data from the platform to satisfy their own regulatory, tax, and audit obligations."},{"term":"Consignment Note","definition":"The transport document (physical or electronic) that constitutes the contract of carriage. EU/UK regulations mandate retention periods for these documents."},{"term":"Right to Erasure (GDPR Art. 17)","definition":"A data subject's right to request deletion of personal data, which does NOT override legal retention obligations under Art. 17(3)(b) for compliance with a legal obligation."}]},"business_rules":[{"id":"BR-1","rule":"Delivery records that constitute or contain tax-relevant transaction data must be retained for the minimum period required by applicable tax law (typically 6-10 years in EU/UK jurisdictions).","invariant":true,"invariant_justification":"UK VAT regulations (VAT Notice 700/21) require 6 years retention. German tax law (AO §147) requires 10 years. French commercial code requires 10 years. These are legal mandates — violation exposes the company and its customers to regulatory penalties. No VP can override statutory law.","testable_assertion":"No delivery record containing tax-relevant data (VAT amounts, invoice references, customs declarations) shall be deleted before the applicable jurisdiction's statutory retention period has elapsed."},{"id":"BR-2","rule":"Proof of Delivery artifacts must be retained for at least the limitation period for contract disputes in the applicable jurisdiction.","invariant":true,"invariant_justification":"UK Limitation Act 1980 sets 6 years for contract claims. Most EU member states have similar periods (3-10 years). Destroying POD evidence before limitation expiry removes the company's and its customers' ability to defend against claims. This is a legal and fiduciary constraint.","testable_assertion":"No POD record shall be deleted while the corresponding delivery's contractual limitation period is still open."},{"id":"BR-3","rule":"B2B customer delivery data must be retained for at least the duration specified in the customer's service agreement, and no shorter than the customer's own regulatory retention requirements as declared at onboarding.","invariant":true,"invariant_justification":"Contractual obligation. Destroying data that B2B customers depend on for their own compliance reporting constitutes a material breach of contract and exposes those customers to regulatory risk. The platform is a data processor acting on behalf of data controllers (the B2B customers).","testable_assertion":"No B2B customer delivery record shall be deleted before the later of: (a) the contractual retention period, or (b) the customer's declared regulatory retention requirement."},{"id":"BR-4","rule":"Customs and cross-border shipment records must be retained per EU Customs Code (UCC) and UK customs regulations — typically 3-7 years depending on the transaction type.","invariant":true,"invariant_justification":"Union Customs Code Art. 51 requires minimum 3 years (extendable by member states). HMRC requires customs records for 4 years minimum. These are statutory obligations that cannot be shortened by internal policy.","testable_assertion":"No customs-related delivery record shall be deleted before the applicable customs retention period has elapsed."},{"id":"BR-5","rule":"GDPR does not mandate deletion — it mandates that data is not kept longer than necessary for its purpose. Where a legal obligation requires retention, GDPR Art. 17(3)(b) explicitly exempts that data from the right to erasure.","invariant":true,"invariant_justification":"Misinterpreting GDPR as requiring aggressive deletion is a common and dangerous error. Deleting data you are legally required to retain is itself a compliance violation. The lawful basis for retention shifts from consent/contract to legal obligation, but the data must be kept.","testable_assertion":"Data retention policy shall never set a retention period shorter than the longest applicable legal retention obligation for that data category."}],"acceptance_criteria":[],"edge_cases":[{"scenario":"A B2B customer is audited by HMRC 4 years after deliveries were made. They request delivery records for tax compliance. Records were deleted at 90 days.","expected_behavior":"This scenario must be impossible. The platform must retain records for the full statutory period. If records are deleted, the platform has caused its customer to fail a regulatory audit — exposing both parties to penalties."},{"scenario":"A consumer disputes a delivery 5 years after the fact (within UK limitation period). The company has no POD because it was deleted at 90 days.","expected_behavior":"The company must retain POD for the limitation period. Without it, the company cannot defend the claim and faces automatic liability."},{"scenario":"A cross-border shipment's customs declaration is requested by EU customs authorities 2 years post-delivery.","expected_behavior":"Records must be available. Customs authorities can audit up to 3-7 years back depending on jurisdiction."},{"scenario":"Mixed data in a delivery record: some fields are personal data subject to GDPR minimization, others are legally required for retention.","expected_behavior":"The system should support field-level or tiered retention — anonymize/pseudonymize personal data where possible while retaining legally required transactional data for the full statutory period."},{"scenario":"A B2B customer's contract expires. They expect continued access to historical delivery data for their compliance obligations.","expected_behavior":"Post-contract data access and retention obligations must be defined in the offboarding terms. Deleting data at 90 days would violate this even after contract termination if the customer's legal obligations extend further."},{"scenario":"Storage cost optimization is achieved through tiered storage (hot/warm/cold) instead of deletion.","expected_behavior":"This is the correct approach — move aged records to cheaper cold storage (e.g., S3 Glacier, archive tiers) rather than deleting them. Achieves the cost goal without violating legal obligations."}],"business_impact":{"financial":"SEVERE NEGATIVE. Regulatory fines under GDPR can reach €20M or 4% of global turnover — but ironically, the risk here is not from keeping data too long, it is from deleting data too soon. HMRC penalties for missing tax records, customs penalties for missing declarations, and breach-of-contract liability to B2B customers could individually reach millions. The storage cost savings are trivial compared to a single regulatory fine or lost enterprise contract.","operational":"CATASTROPHIC for B2B relationships. B2B logistics companies rely on this data for their own compliance. Destroying it makes the platform unusable for any compliance-conscious enterprise customer. This would trigger mass contract terminations and make the platform unsellable to regulated industries.","user":"B2C consumers lose the ability to reference past deliveries for warranty claims, insurance, and disputes. B2B customers lose their compliance data source — a critical dependency. Trust destruction is irreversible.","risk":"CRITICAL. This change, as proposed, would simultaneously violate: (1) UK tax record retention laws, (2) EU tax record retention laws, (3) UK/EU customs record retention requirements, (4) contractual obligations to B2B customers, (5) potential litigation hold obligations. The framing as 'no business logic changes' is dangerously incorrect — data retention IS business logic in a regulated industry.","stakeholders_affected":[{"group":"B2B Logistics Customers","impact":"CRITICAL — lose access to delivery data needed for their own tax, customs, and regulatory compliance. Constitutes material breach of service agreements. Will trigger contract terminations and potential litigation."},{"group":"B2C Consumers","impact":"HIGH — lose ability to reference delivery records for disputes, warranty claims, insurance claims, and chargebacks beyond 90 days."},{"group":"Finance / Tax Team (Internal)","impact":"CRITICAL — company's own VAT records, revenue recognition evidence, and audit trail destroyed. The company itself cannot pass a tax audit."},{"group":"Legal / Compliance Team (Internal)","impact":"CRITICAL — litigation hold violations, inability to respond to regulatory inquiries, destruction of evidence that may be subject to legal preservation obligations."},{"group":"Operations / Customer Support","impact":"HIGH — cannot investigate historical delivery issues, cannot resolve escalated complaints, cannot perform root cause analysis on systemic delivery problems."},{"group":"Sales / Account Management","impact":"HIGH — enterprise prospects will reject the platform during due diligence when they learn retention is 90 days. Competitive disadvantage against platforms offering compliant retention."},{"group":"Engineering Team","impact":"MODERATE — the underlying goal (reduce storage costs) is valid but the solution is wrong. Tiered storage architecture is the correct technical approach."}]},"confidence":{"level":25,"reasoning":"Confidence is extremely low because the proposal as stated is almost certainly illegal in its target markets. The spec frames a legally-constrained business requirement as a pure infrastructure decision, which indicates a fundamental misunderstanding of the domain. There is no evidence that Legal, Compliance, Tax, or the B2B customer success team were consulted. The 90-day figure appears to have no legal basis and contradicts every applicable retention statute I can identify for EU/UK logistics operations.","high_confidence_areas":["UK and EU tax retention requirements exceed 90 days","B2B customers depend on delivery data for compliance","Proof of Delivery has legal significance beyond 90 days","Customs records have statutory retention requirements","Tiered storage is the correct cost-optimization approach"],"low_confidence_areas":["Exact retention periods per jurisdiction (vary by member state — need legal counsel per market)","Specific contractual retention terms in existing B2B agreements","Whether any delivery record categories genuinely have no retention obligation beyond 90 days","Current storage cost breakdown and potential savings from tiered storage vs deletion"],"assumptions":["The platform processes VAT-relevant transactions","B2B customer contracts include data retention commitments","The platform handles cross-border EU/UK shipments","No legal review was conducted before this proposal was made"]},"escalations":[{"type":"contradiction","description":"The proposal is framed as 'no business logic changes' but data retention policy IS business logic in regulated logistics. A 90-day retention period directly contradicts UK VAT retention requirements (6 years), EU member state tax laws (6-10 years), customs record requirements (3-7 years), and almost certainly contradicts contractual obligations to B2B customers. This is not an infrastructure optimization — it is a compliance-destroying change disguised as one.","affected_stakeholders":["B2B Logistics Customers","Finance/Tax Team","Legal/Compliance Team","B2C Consumers","Sales/Account Management"],"options":["A: Reject the 90-day deletion proposal entirely and maintain 7-year retention","B: Implement tiered storage (hot/warm/cold) to reduce costs while maintaining full retention compliance","C: Conduct a formal legal review per jurisdiction to establish minimum retention floors, then implement data lifecycle management with per-category retention periods and tiered storage"],"recommendation":"Option C is the correct long-term approach, with Option B as an immediate cost-reduction measure. Option A (status quo) is safe but does not address the legitimate storage cost concern. The 90-day deletion proposal (as stated) must be rejected — it is not a viable option in any form."},{"type":"missing_context","description":"No evidence that Legal, Compliance, Tax, or Customer Success teams have reviewed this proposal. No analysis of existing B2B contractual retention obligations. No jurisdiction-by-jurisdiction legal review of minimum retention periods. No assessment of active litigation holds.","affected_stakeholders":["Legal/Compliance Team","B2B Logistics Customers","Finance/Tax Team"],"options":["Proceed with engineering assessment only","Pause until Legal and Compliance provide retention floor requirements per data category and jurisdiction"],"recommendation":"Pause until Legal and Compliance provide written retention requirements. Engineering should scope a tiered storage architecture in parallel, but no deletion policy should be implemented without legal sign-off."}],"rejection_reasons":[{"violation":"90-day auto-deletion violates statutory tax record retention requirements in all EU/UK operating jurisdictions","business_rule_broken":"BR-1"},{"violation":"90-day auto-deletion destroys Proof of Delivery before contractual limitation periods expire, removing legal defense capability","business_rule_broken":"BR-2"},{"violation":"90-day auto-deletion breaches B2B customer contracts that depend on delivery data for compliance reporting","business_rule_broken":"BR-3"},{"violation":"90-day auto-deletion violates EU Customs Code and UK customs record retention requirements","business_rule_broken":"BR-4"},{"violation":"The proposal mischaracterizes a compliance-critical policy change as a no-impact infrastructure optimization, indicating insufficient domain analysis","business_rule_broken":"BR-5"}]}
